title: Topic 408: The Psychology of Political Games: Civic Engagement Through Play
content:
# Topic 408: The Psychology of Political Games: Civic Engagement Through Play

## ABSTRACT

This paper navigates the curious intersection of political science and video games, a nexus where “civic duty” and “button-mashing” are no longer mutually exclusive. We explore the psychological underpinnings of political games, from grand strategy epics that let you nuke your political opponents (in a strictly virtual sense, of course) to mobile apps that gamify the thrilling act of voting. Through a rigorous, albeit occasionally satirical, analysis of existing literature and real-world examples, we argue that political games, in all their pixelated glory, may be a surprisingly effective tool for fostering civic engagement. Or, at the very least, they provide a more entertaining alternative to watching cable news. This paper will delve into the minds of players, examining the motivations, cognitive biases, and behavioral shifts that occur when politics becomes a playground. We’ll consider whether these games are creating a new generation of informed, engaged citizens or simply a legion of armchair presidents with an unhealthy obsession with resource management. The findings, we believe, will be of interest to academics, policymakers, and anyone who has ever rage-quit a game of *Civilization* after Gandhi dropped a nuke on them.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand theater of political discourse, where impassioned speeches and heated debates have historically taken center stage, a new player has entered the arena: the video game. Once relegated to the basements of teenagers and the fringes of popular culture, video games have emerged as a formidable force, shaping not only our leisure time but also, we argue, our political consciousness. This paper, therefore, embarks on a noble quest: to understand the psychology of political games and their role in shaping civic engagement. Or, to put it in terms a gamer might understand, we’re here to find out if playing *Democracy 3* can level up your real-world citizenship stats.

The very notion of “political games” might conjure images of backroom deals and Machiavellian maneuvering, but we’re talking about a different kind of game entirely. We’re talking about the digital realms where players can build nations, run for office, and even navigate the treacherous waters of international diplomacy, all from the comfort of their ergonomic gaming chairs. These are the games that allow you to experience the thrill of a presidential campaign without the pesky need to shake hands with actual babies, and the weight of a policy decision without the soul-crushing bureaucracy.

But are these games merely a form of escapism, a digital distraction from the often-messy reality of politics? Or do they represent a new frontier of civic education, a playful and interactive way to engage with the complexities of governance? This paper will argue for the latter, asserting that political games, with their intricate systems and compelling narratives, can serve as powerful tools for fostering political literacy, encouraging critical thinking, and even inspiring real-world action. We will explore the psychological mechanisms at play, from the persuasive power of interactive storytelling to the motivating force of gamified incentives. We will also, of course, take a moment to appreciate the sheer absurdity of a world where a virtual election can feel more consequential than a real one. So, grab your controller, suspend your disbelief, and let’s dive into the fascinating, and often hilarious, world of political games.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the subject of video games and their societal impact. While much of the early discourse was dominated by a moral panic rivaled only by the introduction of rock and roll, a more nuanced conversation has begun to emerge. Researchers, perhaps after a long night of playing *StarCraft* themselves, have started to consider the possibility that video games might not be the harbinger of societal collapse after all. In fact, some have even dared to suggest that they might be, dare we say, beneficial.

A seminal, if now slightly dated, study by the Pew Research Center in 2008, titled "Teens, Video Games and Civics," was one of the first to seriously explore the link between gaming and civic engagement [1]. The study, which surveyed a nationally representative sample of American teens, found that while the sheer quantity of game play was not a strong predictor of civic engagement, the *characteristics* of that play were. Teens who engaged in "civic gaming experiences" – such as helping other players, learning about societal problems, or making decisions about how a community should be run – reported higher levels of civic and political engagement. This suggests that it’s not about how much you play, but how you play the game. Or, to put it in academic terms, it’s not the screen time, it’s the screen *quality*.

More recent scholarship has built upon this foundation, exploring the concept of "gamification" in the political sphere. Hassan (2020) provides a comprehensive review of the literature on the gamification of e-participation, finding that the application of game-like elements to political processes can lead to increased engagement, motivation, and even enjoyment [2]. Who knew that earning badges for voting could be more motivating than a sense of civic duty? Apparently, a lot of people. Masser and Mory (2018) further explore this idea, examining how gamification can be used to encourage citizen participation in policymaking [3]. Their work suggests that by reframing civic tasks as a game, we can tap into our innate human desire for achievement, competition, and, of course, shiny virtual rewards.

Of course, not all scholars are so optimistic. Some have raised concerns about the potential for political games to oversimplify complex issues, reinforce existing biases, or even promote a cynical and detached view of politics. Manuwa (2022), for instance, warns that some political simulations may inadvertently reinforce stereotypes and prejudices [4]. And Loh (2019) questions the long-term effects of gamified participation, wondering if it might lead to a more superficial and less meaningful form of engagement [5]. These are valid concerns, to be sure. After all, if your only understanding of international relations comes from a game of *Risk*, you might be inclined to believe that all geopolitical conflicts can be resolved by rolling a handful of dice. But as we will explore in the following sections, the potential benefits of political games may well outweigh the risks, provided we approach them with a healthy dose of critical thinking and a good sense of humor.

## ANALYSIS

To truly understand the power of political games, we must venture into the labyrinthine corridors of the human mind, a place where logic and emotion engage in a perpetual, and often comical, dance. It is here, in the realm of cognitive biases and psychological heuristics, that the true magic of political games unfolds. By tapping into our fundamental human drives, these games can transform the mundane act of civic participation into a thrilling and addictive experience. It’s like covering your vegetables in cheese; suddenly, something that’s good for you becomes something you actually want to consume.

One of the key psychological principles at play is the concept of **self-determination theory**, which posits that humans have three innate psychological needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Political games, it turns out, are masters at satisfying these needs. The need for **autonomy** is fulfilled by giving players the freedom to make their own choices, to shape their own virtual nations, and to lead their digital citizens to glory or ruin. Whether you’re a benevolent dictator in *Tropico* or a crusading president in *The Political Machine*, the feeling of being in control is a powerful and intoxicating one. It’s like being the master of your own little universe, a universe where you can raise taxes, declare war, and build a giant golden statue of yourself without having to answer to a pesky electorate.

The need for **competence** is addressed through the game’s challenges and feedback systems. As players learn the intricacies of the game, as they master the art of diplomacy and the science of economics, they experience a sense of mastery and accomplishment. This is the same feeling you get when you finally beat a difficult level in *Dark Souls*, but instead of slaying a giant demon, you’ve successfully passed a piece of legislation. The sense of achievement is just as real, and the bragging rights are arguably more impressive at dinner parties.

Finally, the need for **relatedness** is met through the social aspects of many political games. Whether it’s forming alliances with other players in *Politics and War* or debating policy on a game’s online forum, these games can foster a sense of community and shared purpose. This is the digital equivalent of the town hall meeting, a place where citizens can come together to discuss the issues of the day, to forge alliances, and to collectively complain about the high price of virtual tea. As the Pew Research Center study found, these social interactions, particularly when they occur in person, are strongly correlated with real-world civic engagement [1]. It seems that the bonds we forge in the digital world can, in fact, translate into a greater sense of connection to the world around us.

But it’s not just about fulfilling our basic psychological needs. Political games also leverage a host of cognitive biases to keep us engaged. The **endowment effect**, for example, makes us place a higher value on things we own. In a political game, this translates to a fierce loyalty to our virtual nation, a nation we have built from the ground up, a nation we have nurtured and protected. We become invested in its success, and we will go to great lengths to defend it from all enemies, foreign and domestic. This is the same reason you’ll argue with a stranger on the internet for three hours about why your favorite sports team is the best. It’s not just a team; it’s *your* team.

The **IKEA effect**, a close cousin of the endowment effect, further reinforces this sense of ownership. This is the cognitive bias that leads us to place a disproportionately high value on things we have partially created. In the context of political games, this means that the more effort we put into building our virtual nation, the more we will value it. Every road we build, every policy we enact, every citizen we educate becomes a testament to our own hard work and ingenuity. It’s the same reason that lopsided bookshelf you built yourself holds a special place in your heart, a place that a professionally assembled bookshelf could never fill.

Of course, we can’t talk about the psychology of games without mentioning the **Zeigarnik effect**, the tendency to experience intrusive thoughts about an objective that was once pursued and left incomplete. This is the reason you can’t stop thinking about that one last side quest you need to complete in *The Witcher 3*, and it’s the same reason you’ll stay up until 3 a.m. trying to balance your virtual budget in *Democracy 4*. The game presents you with a series of unresolved tasks, a series of problems that need to be solved, and your brain, in its infinite and often frustrating wisdom, will not rest until those tasks are completed.

By understanding these psychological principles, we can begin to see how political games can be so effective at capturing our attention and motivating our behavior. They are not simply a form of entertainment; they are finely tuned machines designed to tap into our deepest psychological needs and biases. And in doing so, they may just be creating a new generation of citizens who are not only informed about the issues of the day, but who are also motivated to do something about them. Or, at the very least, they’re giving us a more entertaining way to procrastinate.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

Lest we be accused of dwelling solely in the realm of theory and abstraction, let us now turn our attention to the real world, a place where the lines between the digital and the political are becoming increasingly blurred. The following examples, we believe, serve as compelling evidence of the growing influence of political games on civic engagement, and as a source of endless amusement for those of us who enjoy a good political satire.

One of the most striking examples of a political game with real-world impact is *iCivics*, a non-profit organization founded by former Supreme Court Justice Sandra Day O’Connor. Yes, you read that correctly. A Supreme Court Justice, a woman who once held one of the most powerful positions in the land, decided that the best way to educate the next generation of citizens was through video games. *iCivics* offers a suite of games that allow players to simulate various aspects of the American political system, from running for president to arguing a case before the Supreme Court. The games are designed to be both educational and entertaining, and they have been played by millions of students across the country. The success of *iCivics* suggests that even the most august institutions of power are beginning to recognize the potential of games as a tool for civic education. It also gives us the delightful mental image of Justice O’Connor, in her judicial robes, yelling at a screen because her virtual candidate lost the Iowa caucus.

Another fascinating case study is the phenomenon of “e-democracy” in Taiwan, where the government has actively embraced gamification as a means of engaging citizens in the policymaking process. The vTaiwan platform, for example, uses a combination of online discussion forums, live-streaming debates, and gamified incentives to solicit public feedback on proposed legislation. Citizens can earn points and badges for their participation, and their input has a real and tangible impact on the laws that are passed. This is a far cry from the traditional model of civic engagement, which often involves sitting through a long and tedious town hall meeting, only to have your concerns ignored by a panel of bored-looking politicians. The Taiwanese model suggests that by making participation more like a game, we can make it more appealing to a wider range of people, and we can create a more vibrant and responsive democracy in the process.

Of course, not all examples of political gaming are so overtly civic-minded. The popular grand strategy game *Crusader Kings III*, for example, allows players to navigate the treacherous world of medieval politics, a world of backstabbing, betrayal, and the occasional assassination. While the game is not explicitly designed to be a tool for civic education, it does an excellent job of illustrating the complexities of power, the importance of alliances, and the often-unforeseen consequences of political decisions. It also, and this is perhaps its greatest contribution to political science, allows you to appoint a horse as your chancellor. This may seem like a silly and frivolous feature, but it serves as a powerful reminder that even the most absurd political scenarios are not entirely beyond the realm of possibility. After all, in a world where a reality TV star can become president, is a horse chancellor really so far-fetched?

Finally, we would be remiss if we did not mention the role of political games in protest movements around the world. In Hong Kong, for example, protesters used the popular augmented reality game *Pokémon Go* to organize and communicate, using the game’s virtual “gyms” and “PokéStops” as rallying points. This is a brilliant example of how a seemingly apolitical game can be repurposed for political ends, and it highlights the creativity and resourcefulness of modern-day activists. It also gives us the wonderfully surreal image of a group of protesters, in the midst of a tense standoff with police, taking a moment to catch a rare Pikachu.

These examples, we believe, demonstrate the diverse and often surprising ways in which political games are shaping our world. They are not just a form of entertainment; they are a new medium for political expression, a new platform for civic engagement, and a new lens through which to view the often-baffling world of politics.

## DISCUSSION

The journey through the pixelated corridors of political power reveals more than just the potential for a new wave of civic engagement; it offers a surprisingly clear window into the soul of the modern consumer. The same psychological hooks that make a game of *Civilization* so addictive are, it turns out, the very same ones that compel us to buy the latest smartphone, to join a loyalty program, or to spend an embarrassing amount of money on virtual hats for our online avatars. By dissecting the mechanics of political games, we can gain a deeper, and perhaps slightly more cynical, understanding of human consumer behavior.

The principles of **self-determination theory**, for example, are not just the bedrock of good game design; they are the holy trinity of modern marketing. The need for **autonomy** is catered to by brands that offer endless customization options, allowing us to feel like we are in control of our purchasing decisions, even as we are being guided by a carefully crafted marketing funnel. The need for **competence** is leveraged by loyalty programs that reward us with points, badges, and tiered statuses, making us feel like we are “winning” at the game of consumption. And the need for **relatedness** is exploited by brands that cultivate a sense of community, creating online forums, exclusive events, and a shared identity around their products. We are not just buying a product; we are joining a tribe.

The cognitive biases that political games so skillfully manipulate are also the bread and butter of the advertising industry. The **endowment effect** is why we are so reluctant to part with our old, outdated gadgets, even when a newer, better model is available. We have invested them with a sense of ownership, a sense of self, and they have become an extension of our own identity. The **IKEA effect** is why we are willing to pay a premium for a meal kit that we have to assemble and cook ourselves. The effort we put into the process makes the final product feel more valuable, more *ours*. And the **Zeigarnik effect** is why we are so susceptible to “limited time offers” and “flash sales.” The fear of missing out, of leaving a task incomplete, is a powerful motivator, and it is one that marketers have learned to exploit with ruthless efficiency.

What, then, are the implications of this for our understanding of civic engagement? If the same psychological principles that drive consumerism can be used to promote political participation, are we simply turning citizenship into another commodity to be bought and sold? Are we reducing the noble act of governance to a series of transactions, a game to be won? Perhaps. But perhaps that is not such a bad thing. In a world where our attention is a finite and increasingly scarce resource, perhaps the only way to make civic engagement appealing is to make it more like the things we already find appealing. Perhaps the future of democracy lies not in a return to some idealized past, but in an embrace of the very tools and techniques that have come to define our modern, consumer-driven world. It is a slightly unsettling thought, to be sure, but it is one that is becoming increasingly difficult to ignore. The game, it seems, is on.

## CONCLUSION

In the final analysis, what are we to make of this strange and wonderful marriage of politics and play? Have we, in our quest for a more engaged citizenry, simply traded the ballot box for the Xbox? The answer, like any good political issue, is complicated. On the one hand, the gamification of civic engagement presents a tantalizing opportunity to reach a new generation of citizens, to make politics more accessible, more engaging, and, dare we say, more fun. On the other hand, it raises a host of thorny questions about the nature of democracy, the meaning of citizenship, and the potential for a more superficial and transactional form of political participation.

But perhaps we are overthinking it. Perhaps the rise of political games is not a sign of the apocalypse, but simply a reflection of the changing times. In a world where we can order a pizza, find a date, and start a revolution all from the same device, is it really so surprising that we would also want to run a country from our couch? The truth is, political games are here to stay, and they are only going to become more sophisticated, more immersive, and more influential. The challenge, then, is not to resist this trend, but to embrace it, to harness the power of play for the betterment of society, and to ensure that the next generation of citizens is equipped with the critical thinking skills necessary to navigate this new and exciting political landscape. And if all else fails, at least we’ll have some entertaining stories to tell at our next virtual town hall meeting.
references:
Pew Research Center. (2008). *Teens, Video Games and Civics*. Retrieved from https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2008/09/16/part-2-video-games-relationship-to-civic-and-political-engagement/

Hassan, L. (2020). A review of the literature on gamification of e-participation. *Government Information Quarterly*, *37*(4), 101534. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.giq.2020.101534

Masser, K., & Mory, L. (2018). *The Gamification of Citizens’ Participation in Policymaking*. Springer International Publishing.

Manuwa, T. (2022). The Role of Political Simulations and Games in Enhancing Political Education and Engagement. *SARJ of Multidisciplinary Disputes*, *2*(3), 23-30.

Loh, W. (2019). The Gamification of political participation. *Moral Philosophy and Politics*, *6*(2), 295-317. https://doi.org/10.1515/mopp-2018-0037
